content.text: Why Naming and Tagging Standards Matter
A metric emitted under the name

in another cannot be aggregated in a single dashboard without bespoke mapping. After three months and a dozen services, the metric namespace becomes an archaeology problem: no one knows which metric measures which thing, tag values are inconsistent across teams, and alert rules drift. Naming and tagging standards prevent this by establishing a shared vocabulary before the metrics proliferate.

For GenAI services the stakes are higher than for typical API services. Token usage drives cost, model identity drives SLO differentiation, and finish reasons distinguish normal completions from budget-exhaustion and safety blocks. Without consistent tag names for these dimensions, cost attribution and safety monitoring break down across service boundaries.

Agree on naming and tagging conventions before the first service ships metrics to production. Renaming metrics after dashboards and alerts are wired to them requires coordinated changes across multiple systems and a transition period with dual-emission — expensive to execute and error-prone.

attrs.alt: Metric naming convention anatomy for GenAI services

content.text: Prometheus-style metric names follow the pattern

. Each segment adds a scoping level from broad (namespace) to specific (what the metric counts or measures). The suffix encodes the metric type so consumers know whether the value is cumulative, a gauge, or a histogram.
Service domain — identifies the owning system
Feature or API surface within the namespace
The thing being counted (omit for dimensionless ratios)

# Counters (_total suffix — always monotonically increasing)
genai_chat_request_total              # requests to chat completion endpoint
genai_chat_error_total                # failed requests by error_type tag
genai_chat_completion_tokens_total    # output tokens consumed (cumulative)
genai_chat_prompt_tokens_total        # input tokens consumed (cumulative)
genai_retrieval_request_total         # RAG retrieval calls
genai_safety_check_total             # safety classifier invocations

# Histograms (_seconds or _duration_seconds suffix — measure latency distributions)
genai_chat_request_duration_seconds   # end-to-end request latency
genai_chat_ttft_seconds              # time-to-first-token (streaming)
genai_embed_request_duration_seconds  # embedding call latency
genai_retrieval_duration_seconds      # vector search latency

# Gauges (current state — may go up or down)
genai_provider_rate_limit_remaining   # remaining rate limit window
genai_cache_size_tokens               # prompt cache size in tokens
genai_model_load_status               # 1=loaded, 0=unloaded (self-hosted)

# Cost metrics (custom, not in OTel spec — use consistently)
genai_cost_usd_total                  # cumulative spend; tag by model, team

Tags (labels in Prometheus, dimensions in CloudWatch, attributes in OTel metrics) slice metric time series so you can filter and group in dashboards. The cardinality of a metric — the number of unique tag value combinations — determines its storage cost and query performance. GenAI services have natural high-cardinality temptations (user IDs, request IDs, prompt hashes) that must never become tags.
Required Tags on Every GenAI Metric

# These tags must appear on every genai_* metric.
# They are the minimum needed to answer "what, where, which model, which env?"

REQUIRED_TAGS = {
    "provider":    str,   # "openai" | "anthropic" | "google" | "self-hosted"
    "model":       str,   # exact model name: "gpt-4o", "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"
    "env":         str,   # "production" | "staging" | "development"
    "service":     str,   # service name: "chat-api", "rag-pipeline", "embed-service"
    "region":      str,   # cloud region: "us-east-1", "eu-west-1"
}

# These tags are strongly recommended — omit only when not applicable.
RECOMMENDED_TAGS = {
    "route":        str,   # HTTP route: "/api/chat", "/api/embed"
    "tier":         str,   # user tier: "free" | "pro" | "enterprise"
    "feature":      str,   # product feature: "search", "copilot", "summarise"
    "team":         str,   # owning team for cost attribution
}
Beyond the required base set, each metric type carries operation-specific tags that enable drill-down during incidents.
Separate stop/length/content_filter counts; length means token budget exceeded
Distinguish timeout vs rate_limit vs auth_error vs model_error
Latency distributions differ significantly between streaming and non-streaming
Cache hit rate and per-index latency are primary retrieval SLIs
action=pass/block/error; track block rate by safety model version
Cost attribution requires team and cost center tags for finance reporting

OpenTelemetry metrics use instruments (Counter, Histogram, Gauge) and attach attributes at record time. A shared instrumentation module ensures every service uses identical attribute keys and value formats.

from opentelemetry import metrics
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics import MeterProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export import ConsoleMetricExporter
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export import PeriodicExportingMetricReader
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional


meter = metrics.get_meter("genai-service", "1.0.0")

# --- Instruments ---
request_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "genai_chat_request_total",
    description="Total chat completion requests",
    unit="1",
)

error_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "genai_chat_error_total",
    description="Total failed chat completion requests",
    unit="1",
)

token_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "genai_chat_completion_tokens_total",
    description="Cumulative tokens consumed in chat completions",
    unit="1",
)

duration_histogram = meter.create_histogram(
    "genai_chat_request_duration_seconds",
    description="End-to-end chat completion request duration",
    unit="s",
)

cost_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "genai_cost_usd_total",
    description="Cumulative cost in USD for GenAI API calls",
    unit="USD",
)


@dataclass
class RequestContext:
    provider: str
    model: str
    env: str
    service: str
    region: str
    route: str
    tier: str = "free"
    feature: str = "chat"
    team: str = "platform"


def record_completion(
    ctx: RequestContext,
    duration_s: float,
    input_tokens: int,
    output_tokens: int,
    finish_reason: str,
    is_stream: bool,
    cost_usd: float,
    error_type: Optional[str] = None,
) -> None:
    """Record all metrics for a single chat completion. Call once per request."""
    base_attrs = {
        "provider": ctx.provider,
        "model": ctx.model,
        "env": ctx.env,
        "service": ctx.service,
        "region": ctx.region,
        "route": ctx.route,
        "tier": ctx.tier,
        "feature": ctx.feature,
        "team": ctx.team,
    }

    if error_type:
        error_counter.add(1, {**base_attrs, "error_type": error_type})
        return

    request_counter.add(1, {**base_attrs,
                             "finish_reason": finish_reason,
                             "stream": str(is_stream).lower()})

    token_counter.add(input_tokens,  {**base_attrs, "token_type": "input"})
    token_counter.add(output_tokens, {**base_attrs, "token_type": "output"})

    duration_histogram.record(duration_s, {**base_attrs,
                                            "finish_reason": finish_reason,
                                            "stream": str(is_stream).lower()})

    cost_counter.add(cost_usd, {**base_attrs, "cost_center": ctx.team})
Enforcing Standards via a Metric Registry

Without enforcement, teams add new metrics with inconsistent names and tags. A metric registry defines the canonical list and validates any new metric against it before it can be emitted in production. This is implemented as a Python module that is imported by all services — the registry is the single source of truth.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Set, Optional
import re


@dataclass
class MetricDefinition:
    name: str
    description: str
    unit: str
    metric_type: str            # "counter" | "histogram" | "gauge"
    required_tags: Set[str]
    optional_tags: Set[str] = field(default_factory=set)


# Canonical registry — import this in every service
GENAI_METRIC_REGISTRY: dict[str, MetricDefinition] = {
    "genai_chat_request_total": MetricDefinition(
        name="genai_chat_request_total",
        description="Total chat completion requests",
        unit="1",
        metric_type="counter",
        required_tags={"provider", "model", "env", "service", "region"},
        optional_tags={"route", "tier", "feature", "team", "finish_reason", "stream"},
    ),
    "genai_chat_error_total": MetricDefinition(
        name="genai_chat_error_total",
        description="Total failed chat completion requests",
        unit="1",
        metric_type="counter",
        required_tags={"provider", "model", "env", "service", "region", "error_type"},
        optional_tags={"route", "tier", "feature", "team"},
    ),
    "genai_chat_request_duration_seconds": MetricDefinition(
        name="genai_chat_request_duration_seconds",
        description="End-to-end chat completion request duration",
        unit="s",
        metric_type="histogram",
        required_tags={"provider", "model", "env", "service", "region"},
        optional_tags={"route", "tier", "feature", "finish_reason", "stream"},
    ),
}

_NAME_RE = re.compile(r'^[a-z][a-z0-9_]*[a-z0-9]$')
_FORBIDDEN_TAGS = {"user_id", "request_id", "session_id", "prompt_hash",
                   "ip_address", "timestamp"}


def validate_metric(name: str, tags: dict) -> list[str]:
    """
    Return a list of violations. Empty list means the metric is compliant.
    Call this in CI tests against sampled metric emissions.
    """
    violations = []

    if not _NAME_RE.match(name):
        violations.append(f"Name '{name}' violates snake_case pattern")

    defn = GENAI_METRIC_REGISTRY.get(name)
    if defn is None:
        violations.append(f"Metric '{name}' is not in the registry — add it before shipping")
        return violations

    for req in defn.required_tags:
        if req not in tags:
            violations.append(f"Required tag '{req}' missing from '{name}'")

    for tag in tags:
        if tag in _FORBIDDEN_TAGS:
            violations.append(f"Forbidden high-cardinality tag '{tag}' on '{name}'")

    return violations

Every tag value combination creates a new time series in your metrics backend. A metric with five tags each having ten possible values generates up to 100,000 series. Most managed metrics services charge by active series or impose hard limits. For GenAI services, model names and providers are naturally low-cardinality, but finish reasons and tier values can multiply quickly if not kept bounded.

from itertools import product
from math import prod


def estimate_series_count(tag_cardinalities: dict[str, int]) -> int:
    """
    Estimate the maximum number of time series a metric can produce.
    In practice not all combinations appear, but this gives the ceiling.
    """
    return prod(tag_cardinalities.values())


# Example: genai_chat_request_total
tag_cardinalities = {
    "provider":      4,    # openai, anthropic, google, self-hosted
    "model":        10,    # 10 deployed models across providers
    "env":           3,    # production, staging, development
    "service":       6,    # chat-api, summarise-api, search-api, ...
    "region":        4,    # 4 deployment regions
    "route":         8,    # 8 API routes
    "tier":          3,    # free, pro, enterprise
    "feature":       5,    # chat, search, copilot, summarise, embed
    "finish_reason": 4,    # stop, length, content_filter, tool_calls
    "stream":        2,    # true, false
}

max_series = estimate_series_count(tag_cardinalities)
print(f"Max series: {max_series:,}")
# Max series: 138,240,000  <-- far too high; prune low-value tags

# After removing "route" and "feature" from request_total:
pruned = {k: v for k, v in tag_cardinalities.items()
          if k not in ("route", "feature")}
print(f"Pruned max series: {estimate_series_count(pruned):,}")
# Pruned max series: 3,456,000  <-- more manageable

Histogram metrics record distributions — request latency, token counts, document sizes. The bucket boundaries determine what percentiles you can compute accurately. Default Prometheus buckets (0.005s to 10s) do not fit GenAI workloads where latency ranges from 200 ms to 120 s for long-context generation. Set explicit buckets matched to the SLO thresholds and expected latency range.

from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics import MeterProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.view import View, ExplicitBucketHistogramAggregation


# Latency buckets for chat completion: cover 200ms to 120s
# Bucket edges correspond to SLO thresholds (p50/p95/p99 targets)
LATENCY_BUCKETS = [
    0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0,
    8.0, 12.0, 20.0, 30.0, 60.0, 120.0
]

# Token count buckets: 100 to 128,000 (max context window)
TOKEN_BUCKETS = [
    100, 256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192,
    16384, 32768, 65536, 128000
]

# Time-to-first-token: 50ms to 5s
TTFT_BUCKETS = [0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0]


latency_view = View(
    instrument_name="genai_chat_request_duration_seconds",
    aggregation=ExplicitBucketHistogramAggregation(boundaries=LATENCY_BUCKETS),
)

ttft_view = View(
    instrument_name="genai_chat_ttft_seconds",
    aggregation=ExplicitBucketHistogramAggregation(boundaries=TTFT_BUCKETS),
)

token_view = View(
    instrument_name="genai_chat_completion_tokens",
    aggregation=ExplicitBucketHistogramAggregation(boundaries=TOKEN_BUCKETS),
)

provider = MeterProvider(views=[latency_view, ttft_view, token_view])

Naming and tagging standards are only enforceable if they are tested. A CI job that captures a sample of metric emissions from an integration test and runs the registry validator catches violations before they reach production.

# test_metric_conventions.py  (run in CI against the integration test server)
import pytest
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export.in_memory_metric_reader import InMemoryMetricReader

# Assume `app` is your FastAPI/Flask app configured with OTel SDK pointing at reader
from myapp import create_app
from myapp.metrics import reader   # InMemoryMetricReader injected at test time
from metric_registry import validate_metric


@pytest.fixture()
def client():
    app = create_app(test_mode=True)
    with app.test_client() as c:
        yield c


def test_all_emitted_metrics_are_compliant(client):
    # Drive a single request to emit metrics
    client.post("/api/chat", json={"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "hi"}]})

    metrics_data = reader.get_metrics_data()
    violations = []
    for resource_metric in metrics_data.resource_metrics:
        for scope_metric in resource_metric.scope_metrics:
            for metric in scope_metric.metrics:
                for data_point in metric.data.data_points:
                    tags = dict(data_point.attributes or {})
                    v = validate_metric(metric.name, tags)
                    violations.extend(v)

    assert not violations, "\n".join(violations)

Metric naming and tagging standards are infrastructure for observability. The naming pattern — namespace, subsystem, operation, unit noun, suffix — produces names that are self-describing and aggregatable across services. The tagging taxonomy separates identity tags (required everywhere) from operational and business tags (added per metric type). Cardinality discipline — never tagging with unbounded values like user IDs or request IDs — keeps storage costs and query performance manageable. A metric registry enforced in CI prevents drift before it reaches production, and histogram bucket configuration matched to actual latency ranges ensures that SLO percentiles are computed accurately.
